mnemonics: No Safety Net: An uncaught exception is like walking a tightrope without a net. If you slip (throw an exception) and don't have a catcher (catch block), you crash (program terminates).
keyPoints: An uncaught exception is one that is thrown during program execution but is not caught by any catch block in the call stack.
When an exception is uncaught, the Java Virtual Machine's default exception handler takes over, which typically prints the exception stack trace and terminates the program.
Unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException and its subclasses) do not require explicit handling, making them more likely to become uncaught if not properly anticipated.
commonMistakes: Assuming a runtime exception (like NullPointerException or ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException) will not occur and therefore not writing code to handle it.
Catching the generic 'Exception' class but not taking appropriate action, effectively silencing the error and making debugging difficult.
Not understanding the call stack and which method is responsible for handling an exception, leading to exceptions propagating up to the main method and terminating the program.
